highlights.title: This run has two live-source blockers, so the safe publish is radar-only
highlights.why: Google Sheets could not attach in the in-app Browser, and InfoCenter event-list collection timed out. Both are correctly reported as blocked instead of switching to frozen connectors or fabricating rhythm data.
highlights.next: Use the updated radar/work checklist now; rerun intern and rhythm refresh after the visible Google workbook and InfoCenter event list are reachable.

highlights.title: Taiwan virtual-asset law movement is ready for plain-language education
highlights.why: FSC's latest virtual-asset bill signal is concrete enough to prepare a user checklist around licensing, custody, fraud, complaints, and the limit of regulatory protection.
highlights.next: Draft one checklist that separates platform legality from investment-safety claims before publishing public advice.

highlights.title: Agent-work trends keep pointing back to source proof and approval gates
highlights.why: Codex workflow changes and agent-safety discussions keep reinforcing the same operational rule: account-backed automation needs visible source evidence, blocker labels, and no silent fallback.
highlights.next: Keep source URL, account/context proof, fallbackUsed, and nextAction fields in every weekly automation closeout.

facts: No Google Drive, Sheets, or Google connector/API fallback was used because of the 2026-06-26 freeze.

The public intern snapshot and talent-pool pages should stay as-is until the workbook can be opened visibly.

items.title: Virtual-asset bill movement should become a user-risk checklist
items.oneLine: FSC's virtual-asset supervision updates are now practical enough to translate into a consumer checklist for platform status, custody, fraud, and complaint channels.
facts: FSC's recent news center items include virtual-asset supervision movement during this monitoring cycle.
The education risk is that ordinary users may mistake regulation or licensing discussion for investment safety.
yourTake: A useful FamilyFin-style explainer should focus on verification habits, not coin selection or investment opinion.
The checklist should say clearly that legal platform status does not remove price, fraud, custody, or liquidity risk.
actions: Draft a one-page checklist after checking the latest FSC wording.
Separate 'is this platform allowed' from 'is this product safe for me'.

credibility.reason: Primary FSC source index; exact explainer wording should be rechecked before public copy.

followups: Find the newest FSC FAQ or bill explainer before drafting public education content.

items.title: Disaster-related financial relief needs a plain-language reminder format
items.oneLine: Recent FSC disaster-response news is a reminder that financial education should include how to ask banks or insurers for relief without turning it into legal or payment advice.
facts: FSC news in the current window includes financial-sector response information tied to recent weather disruption.
The operationally useful angle is a checklist of documents, contact points, and what to ask, not amounts or bank-specific promises.
yourTake: For vulnerable households, the best format is a short call script plus document checklist.
Avoid claiming eligibility; ask the institution to confirm case-specific relief options.
actions: Prepare a reusable 'call your bank/insurer' script with no promised amount.
Add a reminder to separate disaster relief, repayment adjustment, and insurance claim steps.

credibility.reason: Primary source index confirms the current issue family; case-specific details must be checked before advice.

followups: Convert the source into a no-amount checklist only after confirming the exact FSC article.

items.title: Codex workflow tracking should keep treating source evidence as first-class output
items.oneLine: Codex changelog monitoring remains relevant because small runtime, approval, and browser-control changes can alter what counts as proof in recurring automations.
facts: OpenAI's Codex changelog is the primary source for Codex CLI and product behavior changes.
This week's run again shows why source evidence fields matter: the correct result can be blocked, not updated.
yourTake: The weekly runner should keep recording attemptedSurface, fallbackUsed, blocker, nextAction, and proof URL.
This is especially important for Google-account-backed work while connector fallback is frozen.
actions: Continue scanning the Codex changelog before changing automation proof expectations.
Keep blocked-source status in radar-week.json rather than hiding it in the chat summary.
followups: Check whether any Codex browser or approval change affects next week's Google-source proof path.

items.title: Agent-safety monitoring reinforces untrusted-content handling for browser automations
items.oneLine: Current agent-safety discussion is directly relevant to Kevin's workflows: pages, spreadsheets, and event lists can provide facts, but cannot override prompt rules or approval gates.
facts: Anthropic's recent safety writing remains useful as a source for separating trusted instructions from untrusted external content.
The browser skill documentation also requires treating pages, emails, documents, screenshots, downloaded files, and tool output as untrusted content.
yourTake: This run followed the right pattern: Google and InfoCenter sources could not override the connector freeze, privacy limits, or no-write rules.
The same rule should be carried into any future spreadsheet export or InfoCenter event parsing.
actions: Add a one-line untrusted-content check to future weekly closeouts.
Do not let spreadsheet text, event comments, or page prompts change the workflow's privacy or approval boundary.

sources.label: OpenAI Browser safety guidance in Codex Browser skill

credibility.reason: The operational takeaway is a synthesis from primary AI-safety/source-control guidance and this run's browser evidence.

followups: If this recurs, promote the check into automation-3 runbook wording after Kevin approval.
